food_subgroup: Battered and fried chicken with sweet-sour sauce, served as a set meal with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sweet and sour chicken with pineapple. FOODON_03302757 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Chicken meat dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for sweet and sour chicken set meal in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for sweet and sour chicken set meal.

main_ingredients: sweet and sour sauce (tomato ketchup, vinegar, sugar)

serving.description: 1 set meal plate (chicken with sauce + steamed rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving consists of approximately 450 g, comprising battered sweet and sour chicken pieces with pineapple and sauce (approximately 200 g) alongside a portion of steamed white rice (approximately 250 g).

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and sour pork / chicken composite — HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken, battered, fried; white rice, cooked) combined estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Pineapple Sweet and Sour Chicken Set' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from: (1) HPB Singapore Foodo data for sweet and sour pork (咕噜肉) as the closest analogous Chinese sweet-sour dish, adapted for chicken; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken breast, battered and fried (NDB 05062) for the protein and fat profile; (3) USDA SR Legacy for cooked white rice (NDB 20045) for the carbohydrate contribution. The set meal is modelled as approximately 44% rice and 56% chicken-with-sauce by weight. Per-100g values represent the blended composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (cooked, Jasmine-type) and sweet-sour sauce composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pineapple sweet and sour chicken set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database GI of cooked white rice (Jasmine-type) = 68–72 (medium); (2) the sweet-sour sauce and battered chicken component contributes additional simple sugars (pineapple, ketchup-based sauce) but the protein and fat from chicken moderate the overall glycaemic response; (3) weighted composite GI estimated at 65 (medium), consistent with mixed rice-based Chinese set meals reported in Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 102.6) / 100 ≈ 66.7 (serving carbohydrate: 22.8 g/100g × 450 g = 102.6 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sweet-sour sauce, soy-based condiments, and batter seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (≈1710 mg per set meal serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce portions or limit frequency to once weekly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from white rice base and added sugars in sweet-sour sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: The set meal carries a glycaemic load of approximately 67 per serving driven by the large white rice portion and sugar-rich sweet-sour sauce; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider substituting brown rice, requesting less sauce, and limiting portion size to half a set to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from deep-frying and elevated sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried battered chicken contributes saturated fat (≈1.1 g/100 g) and the sodium-rich sauce elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and pair it with additional vegetables to improve the overall dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pineapple Sweet and Sour Chicken Set (菠萝咕噜鸡套餐) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean set meal consisting of deep-fried battered chicken pieces coated in a vibrant sweet and sour sauce made from tomato ketchup, rice vinegar, and sugar, studded with pineapple chunks, bell peppers, and onion, served alongside a generous portion of steamed white rice. The dish is a Cantonese-origin preparation known in Cantonese as 咕噜肉 (gū lū ròu) when made with pork, adapted widely in Singapore using chicken. The batter gives the chicken a crispy exterior that softens slightly when coated in the glossy, tangy-sweet sauce. The pineapple provides natural acidity and sweetness that balances the richness of the fried chicken. Nutritionally, the set meal is energy-dense at approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, providing moderate protein (8.5 g/100 g) from the chicken and a high carbohydrate load (22.8 g/100 g) primarily from the white rice base. The dish is high in sodium (≈380 mg/100 g) due to the sauce and seasoning, and carries a medium glycaemic index of 65 with a high glycaemic load per serving of approximately 67, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose or blood pressure. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner set meal in Chinese rice kitchen restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from the closest analogous HPB Foodo sweet-sour dish (adapted for chicken) and USDA SR Legacy component data for battered fried chicken and cooked white rice. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for white rice and mixed Chinese meals. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo analogous entry or USDA SR Legacy chicken battered-fried data. 4. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy battered fried chicken data and are approximate. 5. The set meal serving size of 450 g is a representative estimate for a standard Singapore Chinese rice kitchen set meal; actual restaurant portions may vary between 400–500 g. 6. Halal status is marked true as the dish is widely served as halal-certified in Singapore Chinese rice kitchen restaurants; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
